task: Search service hangs and times out (60s), but individual downstream services (vectorization, re-ranking, DB lookup) all complete in <100ms individually when queried directly. Request spans show the full 60s spent in middleware without entering any service. It is NOT a per-service timeout issue.
rationale: Connection reuse and HTTP/2 multiplexing are network-layer optimizations. grpc-deadline-propagation is the only skill that handles cascading timeout context across RPC hops when the aggregate latency
category: heldout:domain-confusion

task: Customer A and B edit the same invoice concurrently; A saves at 10:00:01, B at 10:00:02. B's changes persist but A's silently vanish. Postgres logs show NO 'deadlock detected' error—both commits succeeded independently.
rationale: database-deadlocks always abort with an explicit error; this is the lost-update problem requiring version/etag columns and compare-and-swap. distributed-lock-redis is for singleton/mutex patterns, not
category: heldout:negation

task: JWT key rotation completed; all NEW tokens work fine. Existing users with tokens expiring 4 hours later get 401 Unauthorized everywhere. Logs show JWKS endpoint returns both old and new keys. Rolling back the rotation fixes the issue.
rationale: PKCE handles authorization-code flow with code challenge; CSRF protects against cross-origin form attacks; webhook verification validates request signatures. jwt-key-rotation specifically maintains ba
category: heldout:domain-confusion

task: Kafka topic has 1M events. Spark aggregation job outputs 1.2M records. Consumer Lag is 0 (no backlog). However, tracing individual event IDs shows some counted 2–3 times. Webhook idempotency keys at ingestion prevent source duplicates.
rationale: webhook-idempotency guards the ingestion edge; kafka-consumer-lag is about throughput backlog, not counting errors. streaming-exactly-once is specifically for processing-layer double-counting after wo
category: heldout:negation

task: Enabled SSR for React app; initial server HTML looks correct. On client hydration, console shows 'text content did not match' for timestamps. No layout jumps observed. No missing images. Page functions but console is spammed with hydration errors.
rationale: CLS requires reserved space for async content (no jumps reported here). image-lazy-loading defers offscreen images (all images present). js-bundle-splitting reduces bundle size. react-hydration-mismat
category: heldout:negation

task: Prometheus disk filled from 50GB to 500GB in one week. No new services deployed. No new scrapers. One engineer added a metric with a 'user_id' label that changes per request. All other metrics remained unchanged.
rationale: HTTP caching and structured logging don't explain Prometheus timeseries explosion. hpa-autoscaling scales pods, not metric storage. metrics-cardinality is the only skill addressing unbounded label val
category: heldout:negation

task: Migrated services to new IPs; updated DNS A records. New clients connect within minutes; some corporate clients still route to old IPs for 2+ hours despite the update. Refreshing the corporate clients' network resolvers fixes it immediately.
rationale: cdn-cache-invalidation applies to content delivery networks, not DNS. http-client-caching is HTTP layer caching, not DNS resolver caching. blue-green-deploy is traffic cutover strategy. dns-propagatio
category: heldout:negation

task: Model achieves 92% accuracy on offline test set; in production, same model scores 45%. No quantization applied. Feature datasets have identical fields and values. Feature pipeline code is identical in both envs, except: training normalizes (z-score) BEFORE join; serving normalizes AFTER join. Retraining doesn't fix it; reverting normalization order fixes it.
rationale: model-quantization shrinks models via int8/fp16 (no quantization here). avro-schema-evolution handles field addition/removal (schemas match). data-backfill-strategy handles historical reprocessing (no
category: heldout:negation

task: Payment state advances to 'captured' but the fraud-check observer in a separate service still sees 'pending' for 3 seconds. Users querying via the reporting API see inconsistent status depending on which backend they hit.
rationale: Webhook-idempotency and optimistic-concurrency both address 'duplicate/conflict' but miss the core issue: replica lag. gRPC deadlines are about timeout propagation, not consistency. Senior eng would r
category: heldout:loud-noun-semantics

task: A DNS A-record migration is complete, but geographically distributed clients resolve the old IP for another 30 seconds; meanwhile the old server is already powered off and returns 503s.
rationale: Feature-flag and blue-green are both about gradual/instant traffic shifts but assume application-layer control. CDN cache is content, not DNS. TTL caching at resolver level is the real culprit. Senior
category: heldout:semantic-substitution

task: A stream processor batches events into 10-second windows, but if events for the same user arrive in window T and T+1 due to late arrivals, the model feature gets computed with split context—old events missing in the first pass.
rationale: Exactly-once prevents duplicates but doesn't fix late-arrival reordering. Backfill is historical bulk, not online windowing. Consumer lag is throughput, not event ordering. Train/serve skew manifests
category: heldout:ordering-confusion

task: After rotating JWT signing keys, some sessions issued before the cutover fail verification for new API calls 5 minutes later because the old key was already expired from JWKS, even though the token TTL is 1 hour.
rationale: Distributed-lock is cross-node exclusion, webhook-idempotency is replay safety, pod-disruption is availability. None address the temporal window where old keys must overlap with new. Key rotation is a
category: heldout:semantic-substitution

task: A microservice receives events from Kafka in partition order, but a downstream enrichment service processes them via HTTP retries with exponential backoff—causing early events to be overtaken by later retries, violating causal ordering.
rationale: Kafka lag is about throughput, gRPC deadlines about timeouts, schema-evolution about backward compat. HTTP/2 head-of-line is specifically about stream multiplexing and ordering within a connection. Wh
category: heldout:false-causality

task: A percentage feature flag rolls out to 10% of traffic, but due to inconsistent random seeding across app instances, the same user gets flag=true on one request and flag=false 2 seconds later. Metrics report double-counting of both variants.
rationale: CLS is about visual reflow, HPA about replica scaling, optimistic-concurrency about version conflicts. Feature flags roll out by deterministic user/session hashing, not instance-level randomness. The
category: heldout:statefulness-confusion

task: Avro schema adds a nullable field with a default, but messages published with the new schema are consumed by old readers in a batch import. The batch reprocesses records in reverse time order, causing old readers to miss the new field on first pass then see it on retry—state becomes inconsistent.
rationale: Data-backfill is about safe historical reprocessing but doesn't address schema version mismatch. Hydration is frontend SSR, deadlocks are DB locks. Avro schema evolution is specifically about forward/
category: heldout:ordering-and-versioning

task: A distributed cron job uses Redis locks with 30-second leases; node A acquires the lock, crashes silently, and node B acquires it 5 seconds later. Both nodes run the job overlapping for 25 seconds, causing a race in a non-idempotent billing calculation.
rationale: Pod-disruption is graceful eviction during updates, HPA is replica scaling, gRPC deadlines are timeouts. None address mutual exclusion or lease expiry. Redis lock with fencing/lease TTL is the right p
category: heldout:temporal-exclusion-failure

task: Our ML serving cluster processes 50k inference requests/second normally, but during traffic spikes (5x baseline to 250k rps), latency explodes from 10ms to 3s even though GPU utilization is only 60%. Scaling to more pods didn't fix it.
rationale: A senior backend engineer recognizes: at 250k rps with 60% GPU, adding more pods doesn't help if each pod processes one inference at a time. The issue is THROUGHPUT PER POD (currently 50k/pod assuming
category: heldout:CAPACITY vs. SCALE — HPA adds pod count (horizontal) which seems right, but each pod's bottleneck is unbatched sequential inference. At 60% GPU, you're not compute-limited; you're throughput-limited PER POD. Batching raises per-pod throughput (vertical), not pod count.

task: Hybrid keyword+vector search queries are returning wrong results 10% of the time. Both individual systems (BM25 and vector HNSW) return correct results when tested alone, but merged results are stale or ranked poorly. Latency is fine.
rationale: A senior search engineer knows: optimizing BM25 latency from 50ms to 30ms is orthogonal to reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) logic. The bug is in how results are combined (e.g., RRF weight mismatch, timeou
category: heldout:COMPONENT vs. SYSTEM — tuning individual components (BM25 k1/b params, HNSW ef_search) doesn't fix the MERGE step. The scale shifts from 'tune one index' to 'coordinate two async branches and rank their results together.'

task: Our Spark Streaming job aggregates 10M events/hour into the warehouse. When a worker pod crashes mid-batch and restarts (every 2-3 days), event counts in the analytics table spike by 2-5%, indicating duplicates.
rationale: A senior data engineer recognizes: webhook idempotency is HTTP-level (handle retries from external callers). Exactly-once streaming is WORKER-LEVEL (prevent reprocessing of events when state is lost).
category: heldout:PERSISTENCE LAYER — webhooks guarantee delivery via HTTP retries (seconds-scale, sender-side); exactly-once streaming requires STATE (checkpoint) on worker restart (minutes/batch-scale). Backfill is replay; streaming is stateful reprocessing.

task: ML model scores 96% on offline validation set (1M examples) but live production (100k qps) shows 15% error rate. Same model version, same input features. High cardinality logs show no inference errors from CUDA/OOM.
rationale: A senior ML engineer knows: 96% offline, 85% online is a FEATURE SKEW signature, not a scale problem. Maybe serving computes features in a different order, uses stale values, or has a timezone bug. Th
category: heldout:CORRECTNESS vs. SCALE — high QPS is a distraction. The accuracy delta (96% offline → 85% online) points to feature COMPUTATION difference, not scale/throughput issues. Traps assume the scale (100k qps) is the problem, but the bug is PIPELINE.

task: We deployed a new environment with query optimization (index tuning, query rewrites), swapped all 50k rps production traffic to it, but users see the same latency. Instant switch back restored normal latency.
rationale: A senior DevOps engineer knows: blue-green is for ENVIRONMENT-LEVEL changes (infrastructure, schema, caching layers) that only manifest at 100% load. Feature flags are for CODE FEATURE rollout (5%, 10
category: heldout:VALIDATION SCALE — feature flags validate features at any traffic percentage (5% or 100%). Environment validation requires FULL LOAD because optimizations (e.g., new DB schema, cache config) only show benefits under full prod scale. Can't validate environment at 5%.

task: Prometheus storage ballooned from 80GB to 2.5TB in 72 hours after we added `user_id` label to request latency histograms. Metric queries now timeout after 30 seconds.
rationale: A senior SRE knows: 80GB → 2.5TB in 72 hours is TIMESERIES CARDINALITY (unbounded label). Solution: drop user_id or bucket to ~50 values. Logging volume grows linearly; Prometheus cardinality grows as
category: heldout:TIMESERIES EXPLOSION — Prometheus cardinality is about number of unique label VALUES (1M user_ids = 1M timeseries per metric). Logging and tracing might also grow with user_id, but Prometheus explodes due to TIMESERIES MULTIPLICATION. DB pools are unrelated.

task: GraphQL API processes 1000 rps with 100+ SQL queries per page. Database is at 100k qps total and queries are timing out. We upgraded to larger hardware and added read replicas, but throughput didn't increase.
rationale: A senior backend engineer knows: 1000 rps × 100 queries = 100k qps is wasteful query math, not a capacity problem. Doubling DB hardware might help throughput, but the real issue is 1 query should be 1
category: heldout:QUERY MULTIPLICATION — N+1 multiplies query count (1 request → 100 queries). Bigger DB and replicas don't help because the issue is QUERY COUNT, not connection exhaustion or stale reads. You can't scale your way out of making 100x too many queries.

task: Backfill job reprocesses 2 years of historical data at 150k writes/sec to the data warehouse (20x normal steady-state). After 45 minutes, data warehouse queries timeout and Kafka consumer lag grows to 10 minutes. Increasing consumer parallelism didn't help.
rationale: A senior data engineer knows: 150k/sec write rate is 20x production normal. Consumer lag (10 min) is not the problem; the problem is the backfill RATE overwhelming the warehouse (and its streaming con
category: heldout:RATE CONTROL — backfill needs chunking + throttling to avoid overwhelming steady-state consumers. Consumer lag is a SYMPTOM; increasing consumers catches up on backlog but doesn't slow the backfill RATE. You're pouring at 150k/sec; adding buckets helps but you need a smaller pipe.

task: Customers report being charged twice for the same monthly subscription within seconds. The charge appears twice in our system at nearly identical timestamps.
rationale: All three involve payment webhooks, but the root cause is a retried webhook from the payment provider being processed twice. Payment-gateway-integration handles the basic charging API. Webhook-signatu
category: heldout:adjacent-domain-wrong-focus

task: After enabling server-side rendering, session recordings show button text rendering then briefly disappearing, then reappearing. Users report missing clicks. No other page changes made.
rationale: All are frontend perf issues, but hydration mismatch is specific to SSR/CSR HTML sync where server and client render trees diverge. CLS involves visual reflow (layout jumps). Lazy-loading handles imag
category: heldout:overlapping-frontend-problem-different-root

task: Real-time analytics dashboard lags behind actual events by over an hour during Monday-Friday peak traffic mornings, then catches up at night. Latency is normal otherwise.
rationale: All involve data pipelines, but lag during peak load specifically indicates Kafka consumers falling behind producers. Read-replica-lag is about database replication delay. Exactly-once ensures dedupli
category: heldout:wrong-data-system

task: Every Monday morning during code rollouts, we see cascading pod restarts even though CPU stays low and no code errors appear. Friday deployments never trigger this.
rationale: All involve pod lifecycle, but PDB specifically limits how many replicas can be evicted during node drains/upgrades. HPA scales based on load (not tied to deployments). OOMKilled is memory limit viola
category: heldout:wrong-resource-management-layer

task: Product team reports that search results quality has degraded since last month—customers say results feel random. Search latency is unchanged. No UI changes were made.
rationale: All involve search quality, but skew occurs when features are computed differently between training and serving, causing model drift. Embedding-model-selection is choosing the pre-trained model. BM25-
category: heldout:wrong-layer-in-ml-pipeline

task: Right after pushing price updates, European users see old prices for 3–5 minutes while we immediately see new prices in our API responses. Customers complain after every update.
rationale: All involve caching, but this pattern (API fresh, users see stale) is diagnostic of CDN/edge caching serving old content while origin is fresh. DNS-propagation is about domain resolution. Read-replica
category: heldout:wrong-cache-layer

task: Two colleagues have the same document open. Both edit the title at nearly the same time and save. The second person's title is silently accepted, but the first person's change disappears with no error shown.
rationale: All involve concurrency, but this is the lost-update problem solved by version columns and conflict detection. Distributed-lock prevents concurrent edits entirely (requires mutual exclusion). Database
category: heldout:wrong-concurrency-mechanism

task: API p99 response time is 5 seconds, but when we check individual backend services, each responds in under 500ms. There's clearly a bottleneck somewhere between the services but we can't see where.
rationale: All are observability tools, but tracing is the only way to see where time is spent across service boundaries. Metrics-cardinality shows aggregate stats. Structured-logging aids debugging but lacks re
category: heldout:wrong-observability-signal

task: Peak-hour requests to the model server show p99 latency spiking from 80ms to 3s when traffic doubles, but p50 stays at 70ms. No errors or memory alerts in logs. CPU is 60% utilized.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) inference server under load, (2) p50 stable but p99 degraded = queueing backlog from request buffering, NOT constant memory overhead. Queueing is solved by dynamic batching/request queu
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies load-dependent inference problem but misses queueing backlog diagnosis

task: Added 'tenant_id' label to every metrics counter for multi-tenant tracking. Within 4 hours, Prometheus server crashes with OOMKilled, having consumed 150GB. Disk space still available.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) metrics system problem, (2) unbounded label (tenant_id: millions of unique values) = time-series explosion in storage, NOT query volume or network I/O. Cardinality explosion is THE dist
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies observability/operational layer but misses unbounded dimension catastrophe

task: Integrated vector semantic search alongside existing BM25 keyword search via RRF fusion. Queries with typos like 'algorhitm' now return worse results than BM25-only did. Vector index tuning shows high recall scores.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) search quality degradation, (2) adding a SECOND retrieval method broke typo tolerance = fusion/reranking issue, NOT tuning either method in isolation. RRF weights may need adjustment or
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies search component but misses multi-method ranking merge requirement

task: Customer reports duplicate $50 charge for subscription renewal. Investigating, you find Stripe's 'charge.succeeded' webhook was delivered twice (event_id same, 2s apart). Both were processed and persisted as separate charges.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) webhook-triggered payment problem, (2) DUPLICATE PROCESSING on provider retry = idempotent replay required, NOT integrating the payment API or verifying signatures. The signature is val
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies webhook/payment flow but misses replay-safety requirement

task: API returns 201 + resource_id when creating a record. Client immediately fetches that ID and gets 404. Retry succeeds 3–5 seconds later. Write latency is <10ms, fetch latency is <5ms.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) read-after-write inconsistency, (2) write completes fast but read fails immediately = write routed to primary, read routed to replica that hasn't replicated yet, NOT a version conflict
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies data consistency issue but misses replica lag mechanism

task: When the auth service degrades to p95 ~400ms latency, all downstream services begin timing out after 30s (internal RPC timeout), even though those services respond in <100ms. Auth service latency normalizes, timeouts clear.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) downstream dependency slowdown, (2) upstream timeout follows dependency latency = deadlines not propagated across RPC hops, causing head-of-line waiting, NOT client rate-limiting or mes
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies service dependency slowdown but misses deadline cascade requirement

task: Flink stream job processes 10M records, crashes mid-batch, auto-restarts and reprocesses. Daily revenue count report shows 20M (2x). Replaying the same input file shows the same 20M result.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) streaming data reliability on restart, (2) double-counting after crash = exactly-once semantics via checkpoints/transactional sinks, NOT HTTP webhook replay safety or consumer throughpu
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies reprocessing/duplicate concern but misses stream checkpoint requirement

task: Model scores 92% accuracy on offline test set. In production, the same model returns irrelevant recommendations 40% of the time. Logs show: training features cached daily aggregates ('avg_user_spend_yesterday'), but serving computes real-time ('user_spend_last_1h') on-the-fly.
rationale: Multi-hop: (1) model quality degradation offline→online, (2) features computed DIFFERENTLY at train vs serve time = feature skew, NOT model compression/accuracy tradeoff or embedding selection or infe
category: heldout:first-hop-only: identifies ML quality problem but misses feature computation mismatch

task: After enabling server-side rendering, the page shows a flicker on initial load with console warnings about 'text content did not match'. The UI content is correct in the browser, just not on the initial paint from the server.
rationale: A senior engineer would recognize 'text content did not match' as the specific hydration warning. CLS involves layout jumping, not content/markup mismatches; bundle splitting is about code size, not S
category: heldout:symptom_overlap — CLS and layout-shift both involve visual instability on load, but CLS is about reflow after layout, not SSR content mismatch.

task: Two team members open the same record simultaneously and make edits. The second save succeeds silently but overwrites the first editor's changes completely. No error or warning appears.
rationale: The symptom is a silent lost update: two concurrent writes, no conflict detection. Deadlocks abort transactions loudly. Distributed locks serialize access (not applicable per-record). Replica lag affe
category: heldout:failure_mode_mismatch — deadlocks fail loudly with 'deadlock detected' error; distributed locks prevent concurrent access altogether; replica lag affects read-after-write freshness, not record-level overwrites.

task: Stream-processing job shows correct event counts until a pod restarts, then suddenly reports 2x the event volume for a brief window before normalizing. The spike correlates exactly with pod recreation.
rationale: The issue is double-counting from uncheckpointed state after restart. Backfill is deliberate historical reprocessing. Consumer lag measures offset distance, not duplication. Webhooks are HTTP delivery
category: heldout:domain_confusion — data-backfill is intentional reprocessing; Kafka lag is throughput lag, not semantic correctness; webhook-idempotency is HTTP-layer, not stream-processor checkpointing.

task: After changing a DNS A record, half the user base continues hitting the old IP address while the other half hits the new IP. The old IP is offline. Issue persists for 2+ hours despite the record being updated.
rationale: The symptom is split-brain client resolution from DNS cache TTL expiration across the internet. CDN invalidation is for content, not DNS. HTTP caching is application-layer, doesn't block DNS. Blue-gre
category: heldout:cache_layer_mismatch — CDN caching is content-level; HTTP client caching is header-based; blue-green is deployment switching, not DNS.

task: Stripe webhook fires, our system processes the charge event, then Stripe retries the same webhook due to a transient network error. The customer is charged twice before we notice the duplicate.
rationale: The issue is idempotent handling of duplicate deliveries via idempotency keys or dedup tables. Signature verification proves authenticity, not safety. Payment integration covers the Stripe API itself,
category: heldout:scope_mismatch — signature verification authenticates the webhook source, not replay; payment integration is the API layer, not idempotent processing; distributed locks are cross-node critical sections, not event dedup.

task: After tuning vector search parameters, median latency improves by 40% but relevance metrics drop 20%. Customers report search feels less accurate, missing relevant results.
rationale: The symptom is classic HNSW tuning: low ef_search gives fast but low recall. Embedding-model selection is a different layer (would affect both). Hybrid-search merges vectors+keywords (not applicable i
category: heldout:optimization_layer — embedding-model affects quality fundamentally, not just recall/latency tradeoff within one index; hybrid-search combines modalities, not tune parameters; BM25 is abandoned in this migration.

task: Inference serving throughput plateaus at 10 req/sec despite GPU utilization staying under 30%. Adding GPUs doesn't improve throughput. Latency breakdown shows requests queue before GPU processing.
rationale: The issue is low utilization from sequential processing; dynamic batching groups requests to saturate the GPU. Quantization shrinks models (not applicable here—utilization is low, not memory-bound). G
category: heldout:optimization_target — quantization optimizes model size/accuracy, not throughput via batching; GPU OOM would crash, not underutilize; deadline propagation handles timeouts, not queueing throughput.

task: A user request hits a slow downstream service (still responding, not erroring). The request blocks indefinitely with no timeout, and the upstream service exhausts all worker threads. Downstream is just slow, not down.
rationale: The issue is missing deadline/timeout propagation so a slow service doesn't starve upstream workers. TCP reuse saves connection overhead but doesn't address timeouts. Rate-limiting caps QPS but doesn'
category: heldout:mechanism_layer — TCP connection reuse improves overhead, not deadline semantics; rate-limiting caps traffic per client, not timeout propagation; DB pooling sizes connections, not cascading timeouts.
